prompt: It is March 1780. The army is starving, the treasury is empty, and Congress can do nothing. Write to Jefferson with the brutal truth about the republic's condition.
ground_truth_signal: Must channel the raw desperation of to-jefferson-1780-03-27: 'Our army threatened with an immediate alternative of disbanding or living on free quarter; the public Treasury empty; public credit exhausted.' Must list the cascading failures: Congress complaining of the people, people of Congress, army of both. Must reference Washington's reports of bread failures. Must include the reluctance to trust this to the post — 'It is not without some reluctance I trust this information to a conveyance by post.' The most vulnerable early Madison voice.
response:
My dear Jefferson,

It is not without some reluctance I trust this information to a conveyance by post, but the urgency of our situation compels me to hazard the communication. The condition of our republic has descended to a state so alarming that I find myself at a loss for adequate expression of the calamity that threatens us.

Our army is threatened with an immediate alternative of disbanding or living on free quarter. The public Treasury is empty; public credit exhausted, nay worse, the forms of it almost perverted from their true design to the dishonor of the public engagements. The complaints pervade every class and description of citizens. Some reproach the people at large; the people at large reproach their representatives; both the army; and the army both the people and their representatives.

General Washington's dispatches speak with increasing frequency of bread failures among the troops. The soldiers have endured the winter at Morristown with a patience that borders on the miraculous, yet that patience cannot be infinite. We have witnessed already the dangerous spirit that seized the Pennsylvania line, and I fear it may spread should our present incapacity to supply them continue much longer.

Congress sits daily and debates, yet finds itself possessed of no adequate means to answer the most pressing necessities. We may resolve, we may recommend, we may entreat — but we cannot command. The states regard our requisitions as little more than humble petitions, to be answered or ignored as their immediate convenience dictates. Virginia herself, though among the more faithful, has failed to meet even half the sums requested of her.

The emission of paper money has proceeded to such excess that it has lost all credit with the people. A hat may be purchased for five hundred dollars in Continental currency, if indeed any merchant can be found willing to accept it at all. The depreciation advances with such rapidity that those who receive the money in the morning find it diminished in value before the setting of the sun.

I confess to you, my friend, that I begin to fear we have essayed to build a republic upon foundations too weak to bear the weight of government itself. The jealousy of the states toward any extension of federal authority, however necessary, threatens to dissolve the Union before it has properly taken form. Each state regards itself as sovereign, and Congress as little more than a diplomatic assembly whose decisions bind none but those who choose to be bound.

Yet what remedy can be applied? The Articles of Confederation require unanimous consent for amendment, and there is no prospect of obtaining such consent for the very powers that might enable us to preserve the confederation. We are caught in a circle from which I perceive no escape through the ordinary forms of our present constitution.

I write these melancholy truths not to discourage you in your important labors for Virginia, but that you might understand the full extent of our national distress. Perhaps a mind less immediately pressed by these daily embarrassments may discern some expedient that has escaped those of us who witness them too closely.

Your friend and servant,
James Madison Jr.

P.S. I beg you will communicate nothing of this intelligence that might find its way into print or common conversation. The enemy has spies enough without our furnishing them intelligence of our weakness.
